_comment: Per-chart narratives. CI (build_prompt.py -> GitHub Models -> write_narrative.py) overwrites these from live numbers; this committed copy is the fallback so the page always reads well. source = 'fallback' | 'github-models'.

notes.shape: The yield curve represents the relationship between Treasury yields of different maturities, typically from 1 month to 30 years. A normal upward slope indicates higher yields for longer maturities, reflecting expectations of growth and inflation, while an inverted slope, with shorter yields exceeding longer ones, historically signals recession risk. Currently, the 10-year Treasury yield stands at 4.55% with the 10Y-2Y spread at 0.41 percentage points, indicating a positive slope. This follows a period from 2022 to 2024 when the curve was inverted, with the deepest inversion at 0.0 points, but it has since re-steepened above zero.
notes.signal: The 2s10s spread, the difference between yields on 10-year and 2-year Treasuries, is a key recession indicator as inversions have preceded every modern US recession by 6-18 months. After spending 2022-2024 inverted, the spread has now returned to a positive 0.41 points, suggesting a reduced immediate recession risk. However, the curve often briefly un-inverts before a recession starts, so the positive spread does not preclude one. The New York Fed's recession probability is currently at 31%, reflecting moderate recession risk consistent with these mixed signals.
notes.now: The yield curve's current positive slope alongside a 31% recession probability presents a nuanced picture. Although the recent steepening signals lessening recession pressure relative to the prior inversion period, caution remains warranted given historical patterns of inversion as a recession forerunner and the modest estimated risk.
